title: allsol2901
body:
UNSW School of Mathematics and Statistics
                                    MATH2901
                             Higher Theory of Statistics
                     T UTORIAL EXERCISES - S OLUTIONS

Contents
1   Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability       2

2   Elements of Probability theory                           7

3   Random variables                                        16

4   Random vectors                                          28

5   Common distributions                                    40

6   Transformations of random variables                     51

7   Introduction to statistical inference                   61

8   Estimation methods                                      67

9   Confidence intervals                                    80

10 Hypothesis testing                                       87
 1   Randomness, Variability, Uncertainty, Probability
1.1. (a) Before the coin lands, the outcome has not yet been determined. Given that there are
         two possible results (sample space = {Heads, Tails}) and no inherent bias favouring one
         over the other (assuming a fair coin and an ideal flip), the outcome is decided totally at
         random. Consequently, each outcome must be assigned an equal probability, meaning
         the probability of landing on Heads must be 1/2 as well as that of landing in Tails (;
         ‘distribution’). These probabilities correspond to the long-run relative frequencies of
         Heads and Tails: if the coin was flipped a large number of times, it would land on Heads
         approximately half the time, and on Tails the other half.
     (b) As reminded above, randomness is defined in relation to the existence of a distribution,
         which describes the overall pattern of all possible outcomes of an experiment. Therefore,
         the concept of randomness is meaningful only when considering possible outcomes yet
         to come, but no more after the experiment has taken place and the outcome determined.
         At that stage, we are past the point where randomness is relevant: the unpredictability
         has been resolved.

         Here, once the coin has landed, the outcome is already determined: it is either Heads or
         Tails, and there is no longer any randomness at play. As a result, the only meaningful
         value we could give to the probability of observing Heads would be: 0 if the coin landed
         on Tails and 1 if it landed on Heads – but we do not know which!

         It is true that we are still uncertain about the outcome, but this stems from a lack of
         knowledge, not from randomness itself. This is an example of epistemic uncertainty,
         which arises from incomplete information, as opposed to aleatory uncertainty, which is
         due to inherent randomness in the process like in part (a).

         Whether or not we are still willing to say: ‘there is a probability of 1/2 that the coin
         under your hand shows Heads’, is largely a philosophical question. There are two main
         perspectives on this issue:

           • The frequentist perspective: a probability is strictly tied to the long-run frequency
             of occurrence of an outcome (like described in part (a)). From this viewpoint, once
             the experiment has been conducted and the outcome determined, the sample space
             of possible outcomes collapses to the single outcome that has actually occurred!
             Since the notion of a ‘frequency of occurrence’ for different possible outcomes is no
             longer meaningful in this context, it is not appropriate to assign probabilities other
             than the trivial values 0 or 1. In particular, a statement like ‘there is a probability of
             1/2 that the coin under your hand shows Heads’ violates the frequentist view.

              The only valid statement we can make is that, had this coin flip been part of a large
              number of similar flips, approximately half would have resulted in Heads. In that
              sense, there is no reason to believe more strongly that Heads lies under you hand,
              than Tails. We usually convey this idea by saying that we can be as ‘confident’ that
              Heads is under your hand as we are that Tails is. This level of confidence (here:


                                                 2
     50%) is what is typically reported; indeed, simply stating ‘the probability is either
    0 or 1 but we do not know which’ would not sound very serious.
  • The Bayesian perspective: the idea that our realisation of the experiment is only
    one instance in a large series of hypothetical identical repetitions is rejected, so the
    ‘long-run frequency of occurrence’ interpretation of a probability becomes null and
    void. Instead, a probability is interpreted as a measure of degree of belief in a given
    statement. From this viewpoint, a probability need not reflect inherent random-
    ness but can instead represent a state of incomplete information. In our scenario,
    although the coin’s outcome has already been determined, we do not yet know it.
    Thus, we can still assign a probability 1/2 to the event that the coin under your hand
    shows Heads, as this quantifies our uncertainty based on the information available.


Now, it can be understood that the distinction between aleatory uncertainty (due to in-
trinsic randomness) and epistemic uncertainty (due to lack of knowledge) is often quite
blurry. For example, we can argue that, in all that matters, the two situations considered
in (a) and (b) are effectively identical: since we have no knowledge of what lies under
your hand, we could just as well ignore that trial, re-flip the coin and only record the
outcome at that point.

Also, the randomness which seems inherent in the process of flipping a coin may arise
largely from our ignorance of the precise physical conditions governing the flip. In prin-
ciple, if we knew the exact initial velocity, angular momentum, air resistance, surface
properties, and even minute factors like the coin’s deformation upon impact or slight
variations in air currents, we could predict the outcome deterministically – but of course,
there is no way we could ever know about all that!

This idea aligns with the concept of Laplace’s Demon, a thought experiment proposed
by Pierre-Simon Laplace in 1814. He imagined an all-knowing intellect (said demon)
that, given complete knowledge of the position and momentum of every particle in the
universe, could predict the future with absolute certainty. From this perspective, ran-
domness is merely an illusion created by our limited knowledge rather than an inherent
feature of reality. In other words, there is no such thing as ‘aleatory uncertainty’, only
‘epistemic uncertainty’. While this view may have some appeal in theory, it is easy to
think of counter-examples.

Finally, let us clarify that the difference between frequentist and Bayesian approaches
is mostly a matter of interpretation. Regardless of the viewpoint one adopts, the funda-
mental mathematical framework developed in the following chapters (probability rules,
distributions, statistical methods, etc.) remains the same. To quote the American math-
ematician and statistician Leonard Savage in ‘The foundations of Statistics’ (1954):

‘It is unanimously agreed that statistics depends somehow on probability. But, as to what
probability is and how it is connected with statistics, there has seldom been such com-
plete disagreement and breakdown of communication since the Tower of Babel. Doubt-


                                       3
          less, much of the disagreement is merely terminological and would disappear under
         sufficiently sharp analysis.’

         In this class, we will take a frequentist perspective, as is standard in probability courses at
         this level, interpreting probabilities in terms of long-run frequencies. However, we will
         not be overly rigid in our approach and may occasionally refer to probabilities in contexts
         where uncertainty arises from incomplete information rather than inherent randomness
         (see Question 1.3 below). This will not affect mathematical rigour at this stage, so there
         is no need to dwell on the philosophical distinction too much – simply being aware of it
         is sufficient. To go further, the UNSW class MATH3871 is entirely devoted to Bayesian
         Probability and Statistics.
1.2. (a) Let us suppose that there are l million (1M) people in the population (any ‘large’ number
         would do). Suppose that they are all tested. Then, amongst the 1M people, about 1M
         × 99% = 990,000 (990K) would not have the disease, of whom about (1M × 99%) ×
         10% = 99K would test positive. Also, about 1M × 1% = 10K would have the disease,
         of whom about (1M × 1%) × 90% = 9K would test positive.

         So, over the whole population, a total of about 99K + 9K = 108K would test positive, of
         whom only 9K would actually have the disease. In other words, only 9/108 = 1/12 of
         those who would test positive actually have the disease. A random person drawn from
         the population and testing positive is actually much more likely not to have the disease
         than otherwise.
     (b) This is akin to the problem studied in 1.1b above. Here there is no ‘randomness’ as such
         in whether or not Homer has the disease: either he does have it or he does not have it – it
         is just that we do not know. From a frequentist perspective, all that we can say to Homer
         is that if every person were tested, then the fraction of those with positive results who
         would have the disease is 1/12; and in this sense he can be 11/12 (≈ 91.7%) ‘confident’
         that he does not have the disease.

         This said, in this class, we will not be overly hesitant to state that Homer has the disease
         with probability 1/12 = 8.3% (I’ll make sure to send him a copy of the lecture notes so
         he can decide what that statement means to him!)
1.3. (a) Since each of the 1,000 pages is equally likely to be removed (‘tear out one page totally
         at random’), the probability that page 42 is the one missing is simply 1/1, 000 = 0.001.
     (b) Yes, this does change the probability that page 42 is missing. Learning that page 500
         is present rules out the possibility that page 500 was removed. It does not make any
         other page more or less likely to be missing, but instead of 1,000 possibilities initially,
         we have now ‘only’ 999 left. So, the probability that page 42 is now missing is 1/999 =
         0.001001. In fact, what we have here is a case of conditional probability: the obtained
         probability of page 4 being the missing one is valid if page 500 is not the one which has
         been removed – i.e., under a given condition.
     (c) Now, the friend has ruled out 500 pages, leaving only the remaining 500 (including
         page 42) as possibilities for the missing page. Since every page between 1 and 500 was

                                                  4
          equally likely to be removed at the beginning, and that remains true, the probability that
         page 42 is missing is now 1/500 = 0.002. Again, this is a conditional probability.
     (d) As discussed earlier, in a strictly frequentist sense, we should refrain from using the
         term ‘probability’ for events that have already occurred but are simply unknown to us
         (such as which page has been torn off). Instead, we would speak in terms of confidence
         levels. From this perspective, stating that ‘there is a probability of 0.001 that page 42
         is missing’ in (a) would not be strictly meaningful. However, here we will take a more
         flexible approach and allow the use of such terminology.

         Conditional probability presents an interesting hybrid case. Once we condition on a
         known event (such as ‘it is not page 500 which is missing’ or ‘it is not a page between
         501 and 1000 which is missing’ in (b) and (c)), we have effectively ruled out certain
         possible outcomes. Yet, in general, more than one outcome remains possible, meaning
         that we are still left with a non-trivial sample space of remaining possibilities, on which
         we may imagine a distribution in the frequentist sense. For example, if we were to repeat
         the process ‘tearing a page totally at random’ a large number of time and keep only the
         cases where the torn page is not page 500, then the long-run frequency of times that the
         torn page would be page 42 is 1/999.

         In this sense, conditional probability acts as a bridge between the frequentist and Bayesian
         perspectives: it retains the frequentist foundation by being defined in terms of limiting
         relative frequencies, yet it also allows us to update our degree of belief in an event after
         conditioning on partial knowledge in what resembles the Bayesian way.
1.4. (a) The coin is fair, so the probability of Heads or Tails on any given flip is 50% – and
         this remains true regardless of past results. The fact that those probabilities remain
         unaffected by previous flips is referred to as independence, which is an essential concept
         in Probability (see Section 2.9). Concretely: the coin does not magically turn into a
         biased coin at some point, it has no memory and does not attempt to ‘correct’ itself. The
         misconception arises from misunderstanding randomness: the distribution captures the
         long-run frequency of occurrence of Heads and Tails, and by long-run we really mean
         ‘in the limit, over an infinite number of repetitions of the experiment’. What happens
         over any finite number of such repetitions cannot alter that limit.
     (b) Another common example of the gambler’s fallacy occurs in lottery numbers. Suppose
         a particular set of numbers (e.g., 5-12-23-34-45-49) has not been drawn in a national
         lottery for years. Some people might believe that this combination is now ‘due’ to
         appear soon, thinking that the longer a number hasn’t appeared, the more likely it is to
         be drawn. However, each lottery draw is independent, meaning that past outcomes do
         not influence future ones. The probability of any specific number combination remains
         the same in every draw, regardless of previous results.

         In financial markets, investors sometimes believe that if a stock has been falling for
         several days in a row, it is ‘due’ for a rebound. In reality, stock price movements are
         influenced by market conditions, news, and economic factors, not by the idea that the
         stock must go up after a losing streak. Assuming a stock will rise just because it has

                                                5
          been declining is a classic example of the gambler’s fallacy; in this case, also known as
         the ‘mean-reversion fallacy’.

         Many sports fans may believe that a basketball player who has missed several free throws
         in a row is ‘due’ to make the next one. In reality, unless there is an underlying change
         (such as player fatigue, or other performance-related factors such as loss of confidence),
         each shot is independent of the previous ones. The player’s probability of making the
         next free throw remains the same as before (assuming no external influences).
     (c) Mathematically, the probability remains 50% for each outcome, assuming the coin is
         fair. However, here our intuition may suggest bias in the coin after such an extreme
         streak. Concretely, such a streak would be so unlikely to occur if the coin was fair, then
         one might suspect the coin to be unfair and consider statistical tests to assess the extent
         of the bias – we’ll study such statistical hypothesis tests in Chapter 10. This is obviously
         a totally different question that those related to the gambler’s fallacy.

1.5. (a) Variability in height measurements arises from genetic differences, environmental fac-
         tors (e.g., nutrition), and measurement errors. When randomly selecting groups, differ-
         ent individuals will be chosen each time, leading to natural variation in the observed
         heights. Thus, we do not expect to see the same values in every sample.
    (b) A 70% chance of rain for tomorrow means that it would rain on 70% of the days exposed
        to identical atmospheric conditions (which the forecast model accounts for). It does not
        mean that it will definitely rain or that rain is ‘due’. A correct forecast does not guarantee
        a specific outcome: if it does not rain, the forecast was not wrong; it simply happened
        that tomorrow was one of those 30% of like days on which it was not to rain! This just
        reflects uncertainty.
     (c) The percentage may fluctuate due to sampling variability, as different individuals are se-
         lected each time. Increasing the sample size reduces this variability, making the observed
         proportion more stable and aligning it more closely with the true population proportion,
         simply because a larger sample better covers the entire population. This is known as the
         Law of Large Numbers, and will be discussed in Chapter 4.

        (e) (A ∩ B ∩ C c ) ∪ (A ∩ B c ∩ C) ∪ (Ac ∩ B ∩ C)

2.3.    (a) If E1 is independent of itself, then P(E1 ∩ E1 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E1 ). As (E1 ∩ E1 ) ≡ E1 ,
            this reduces to
                                                P(E1 ) = (P(E1 ))2 ,
            so that P(E1 ) can only be 0 or 1.
        (b) If P(E1 ) = 0, then for any other event E2 , 0 ≤ P(E1 ∩ E2 ) ≤ P(E1 ) = 0, so
            P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = 0 also. Thus,
                                 P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = 0 × P(E2 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E2 ),
            and E1 and E2 are independent.
            For any E2 , P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1c ∩ E2 ). But if P(E1 ) = 1, P(E1c ) = 0 and
            0 ≤ P(E1c ∩ E2 ) ≤ P(E1c ) = 0, implying P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ). Hence P(E1 ∩ E2 ) =
            1 × P(E2 ) = P(E1 ) × P(E2 ). E1 and E2 are independent.
2.4. Clearly,
                                                                     ind.
                      P(E1c ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1 ∩ E2 ) = P(E2 ) − P(E1 )P(E2 )
                                   = P(E2 )(1 − P(E1 )) = P(E1c )P(E2 )

     ; E1c and E2 are independent.
    For the second part, apply the first part to the pair of events E2 , E1c .

2.5. From Bayes’ first rule we can write
                                         P(E1 |E2 )   P(E2 |E1 )
                                                    =            .
                                          P(E1 )       P(E2 )
    Hence, if the left-hand side is greater than 1, the right-hand side must be so.

2.6. Let M denote the event “the randomly chosen worker is married” and G denote “the ran-
     domly chosen worker is a university graduate”. We are given

                           P(M ) = 0.72,      P(G) = 0.44,      P(M |G) = 0.5.

      (a) We have:

            P(neither married nor a university graduate) =       P(M c ∩ Gc )
                                                         =       P((M ∪ G)c ) = 1 − P(M ∪ G)
                                                         =       1 − {P(M ) + P(G) − P(M ∩ G)}
                                                         =       1 − {P(M ) + P(G) − P(M |G)P(G)}
                                                         =       1 − (0.72 + 0.44 − 21 × 0.44) = 0.06


      (b)

            P(married but not a university graduate) =      P(M ∩ Gc ) = 1 − P((M ∩ Gc )c )
                                                     =      1 − P(M c ∪ G)
                                                     =      1 − {P(M c ) + P(G) − P(M c ∩ G)}
                                                     =      1 − {P(M c ) + P(G) − P(M c |G)P(G)}
                                                     =      1 − (0.28 + 0.44 − 0.5 × 0.44) = 0.5


      (c)

                                 P(married or a university student) = P(M ∪ G)
                                     = 1 − P(M c ∩ Gc )
                                     = 1 − 0.06 = 0.94.


2.7. Let R be the event that a person is promoted, and M be the event that they are male. For a
     randomly selected worker, we are given:

                       P(R ∩ M ) = 0.17 P(Rc ∩ M ) = 0.68 P(M ) = 0.85
                      P(R ∩ M c ) = 0.03 P(Rc ∩ M c ) = 0.12 P(M c ) = 0.15

     Now using this information,

    while
                      P(R) = P(R ∩ M ) + P(R ∩ M c ) = 0.17 + 0.03 = 0.2.
    Therefore, R and M are independent (P(R|M ) = P(R)); and there is no gender bias in
    promotion at that workplace.

 2.10. Let trial i consist of the (2i − 1)th and the (2i)th points. So, trial 1 consists of the first two
      points, trial 2 of the next two points, and so on. During each trial, two things can happen:
      either a player (A or B) wins the two points, and the game is over, or each player wins
      one point, and the game starts again from the initial situation. The former happens with
      probability p2 + (1 − p)2 , while the latter happens with probability 2p(1 − p). A total of 2n
      points (i.e., n trials) are played if the first (n − 1) trials all result in each player winning one
      point, and then one player winning both points during the nth trial. By independence,

                        P(2n points are needed) = (2p(1 − p))n−1 (p2 + (1 − p)2 )

2.11. On each toss, the sum of the faces is 3 with probability 2/36, 7 with probability 6/36 and
      neither 3 nor 7 with probability 1 − 2/36 − 6/36 = 28/36.

2.13.    (a) Let A be the event “Urn 1 is selected” and B be the event “ball is red”. Then A and Ac
             partition the sample space and by the Law of Total Probability:

                                      P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|Ac )P(Ac )
                                           = 25 × 12 + 49 × 12
                                           = 19

2.15. Let T = {Driver tested}, D = {Driver is drunk}. We are given:

                          P(T |D) = 0.8,      P(T c |Dc ) = 0.8,   P(D) = 0.12.

       (a) P(T |Dc ) = 1 − P(T c |Dc ) = 1 − 0.8 = 0.2.
       (b) By the Law of Total Probability:

                                   P(T ) = P(T |D)P(D) + P(T |Dc )P(Dc )
                                         = 0.8 × 0.12 + 0.2 × 0.88
                                         = 0.272

                                                           

3.1. We act sequentially as follows:

    then, using the fact that rounds are independent of each other:

             pX (2) = P(X = 2) = P(winner found in second round)
                    = P((same items in first round) ∩ (different items in second round))
                    = P(same items in first round) P(different items in second round)
                    = 31 × 23


    and

          pX (3) = P(X = 3) = P(winner found in third round)
                 = P((same items in first two rounds) ∩ (different items in third round))
                 = 13 × 31 × 23 .

3.2. The graph should be like:

        • The curve is right skewed;
        • The density is 0 for x less than 40 minutes, and (almost) 0 for x greater than 120
          minutes;
        • The curve should have its ‘centre of gravity’ close to 60;
        • The area under the curve less than 52 minutes is indicated to be 0.25;
        • One mode is expected. If a multi-modal distribution is proposed, plausible reasons for
          this should be given.

 3.3. The sample space S consists of 24 = 16 equally likely outcomes.

      (a) Let X be the number of heads before the first tail. What happens after the first tail is
          irrelevant, so we stop recording as soon as a tail appears (or after four heads if no tail
          appears). We compute P(X = x) by considering the first appearance of a tail:
            – X = 0: T... (tail on first flip): 1/2.
            – X = 1: H then T...: 1/2 · 1/2 = 1/4.
            – X = 2: HH then T...: (1/2)3 = 1/8.
            – X = 3: HHH then T: (1/2)4 = 1/16.
            – X = 4: HHHH (no tail): (1/2)4 = 1/16.
          So the pmf of X is:
